summary: Gruber reviews the Spaces improvements in Mac OS X 10.5.3, which finally address his longstanding frustration with the feature. Since Leopard's launch, Spaces forced users into app-based partitioning rather than task-based partitioning, automatically jumping between spaces when switching apps. A new checkbox in 10.5.3 allows users to stay in their current space when switching apps, enabling the task-oriented workflow Gruber wanted. He details the nuanced behavior of Dock clicks, Command-Tab, and a Safari-specific issue with tabbed link opening. He praises the change while noting the checkbox label is unclear and help documentation wasn't updated.
key_points: Spaces in 10.5.0-10.5.2 was designed for app partitioning, not task partitioning, making it impossible to group windows from different apps in one space without unwanted space-switching
A new checkbox in 10.5.3 ('When switching to an application, switch to a space with open windows for the application') fixes this when unchecked, keeping users in their current space
Clicking a Dock icon multiple times cycles through spaces containing that app's windows, providing a deliberate way to navigate between spaces
Safari's tab preference interacts poorly with the new feature — opening links from other apps in a space without Safari windows still jumps spaces unless set to open in new windows
The default behavior remains unchanged in 10.5.3, so existing users won't notice differences unless they opt in
money_quotes: Spaces seems designed for app partitioning, not task partitioning.
Once you're in a space, you stay there until you explicitly switch spaces, not just switch apps. This makes all the difference in the world.
This is a major change to the way Spaces works, but the checkbox label doesn't exactly make it clear.
I've tried to love Spaces but can't, because I want to divide spaces into tasks, and some apps, like my web browser, need to have windows in every space.

Spaces really only seemed suited to putting all of any given app's windows in a particular space. This isn't to say Spaces wasn't usable, only that it wasn't usable for grouping a few windows from different apps together in one Space.
If you turn this new checkbox off, however, activating an app, even one that has no windows in the current space, will not jump you to another space.
key_insight: Apple's Spaces feature was fundamentally limited by its app-centric design until 10.5.3 added a single checkbox that finally enabled the task-based workflow power users actually needed.
